TRINITY · implicit→momentum transition trigger

Why does the hybr run never leave the energy phase?

A clean-room investigation — certify the substrate, then characterise. Identify the mechanism, test five trigger ideas, run the chosen reading across six regimes, measure, and prototype the root fix. Verified 2026-06-20; all artifacts committed under docs/dev/transition/cleanroom/.

TL;DR. Under the default solver betadelta_solver=hybr, every run stalls in the implicit energy phase and never reaches momentum (0/6 configs transition; legacy reached it 6/6). The hand-off fires on a single criterion, \((L_{\text{gain}}-L_{\text{loss}})/L_{\text{gain}} < 0.05\), which never trips. We first certified the substrate (the hybr solver + the \(\beta,\delta,P_b,E_b\) machinery the trigger reads) with an independent gate — it is sound, so hybr exposed real behaviour, not a bug. Measuring the retained-energy fraction \(f_{\text{ret}}=E_b/\!\int\!L_{\text{mech}}\,dt\) against 3D-sim / observed bands shows unanimous under-cooling (\(f_{\text{ret}}\) plateaus at 0.25–0.40, never the observed 0.01–0.1). Harvesting every candidate trigger shows no cooling/force family ever fires — the only physical end-of-energy-phase is geometric blowout (\(R_2>r_{\text{cloud}}\)). Verdict: the stall is a physics-completeness signal, not a mis-tuned threshold — the trigger tests for an event that does not occur. The root fix (mixing-layer cooling, magnitude \(\theta\!\approx\!0.25\)) is the right direction but a naive bulk energy sink stalls the solver, so it was reverted; production is unchanged (pytest 557 passed).

The chain of reasoning

Each step answered one question and set up the next. This is the spine of the report; every link is backed by a figure and a table below, and every number traces to a committed CSV in data/.

1 · Did the new hybr solver introduce a bug, or expose real behaviour?
Real behaviour. An independent substrate gate (C0) passes: the solver drives its temperature residual to \(\le\!0.13\%\) and the \(\beta\!\leftrightarrow\!dP_b/dt\) residual is finite-difference truncation (shrinks \(\propto\!\Delta t\)). hybr finds the true root.
▼
2 · If the substrate is sound, why does the trigger never fire?
The bubble retains too much energy to ever balance: \(f_{\text{ret}}\) plateaus at 0.25–0.40 and never enters the observed 0.01–0.1 band. The modelled interior under-cools.
▼
3 · Is it just the metric form (the instantaneous rate-ratio)?
No. The cumulative-cooling (any \(\eta\)), cooling-timescale and force-balance families were harvested too — the cumulative cooling never reaches \((1-\eta)\!\int\!L_{\text{gain}}\) either. Not a metric-form bug.
▼
4 · Then is there any cooling transition to trigger on?
No. \(E_b\) grows monotonically to t=6 in 5/6 configs — there is no \(E_b\)-peak to track. The only family that fires at a physical epoch is blowout (\(R_2>r_{\text{cloud}}\)), whose epoch is purely geometric (0.01–3.66 Myr \(\propto\) cloud size).
▼
5 · Can we restore a cooling transition by adding the missing physics?
The magnitude is right — a mixing-layer sink \(\theta\!\approx\!0.25\) brings \(f_{\text{ret}}\) into the observed band offline — but a naive bulk \(dE_b/dt\) sink drives \(dM/dt<0\) and stalls the solver. Reverted: it must be integrated into the structure solve.

Conclusion: substrate sound → the bubble under-cools → no cooling family ever fires → the transition is geometric, not thermal → so the rate-ratio trigger isn't mis-tuned, it tests for an event that does not occur. Pragmatic fix = a blowout trigger; root fix = the cooling physics (mixing layer), integrated into the structure solve.

1 · Identify the problem — the mechanism, not the symptom

TRINITY walks a feedback bubble through four phases: energy → implicit energy-driven → transition → momentum. The implicit→momentum hand-off is decided by a single criterion (run_energy_implicit_phase.py:1095): switch once radiative cooling has nearly caught up with the instantaneous mechanical power, \[ \frac{L_{\text{gain}}-L_{\text{loss}}}{L_{\text{gain}}} < 0.05 . \] Under the new default hybr solver this ratio plateaus near 0.5 and never approaches 0.05, so all six configs sit in implicit until the 15 Myr cap — 0/6 reach transition or momentum.

The ratio's shape has two distinct features with different causes — we decomposed both into \(L_{\text{gain}}\) and \(L_{\text{loss}}\) (verified, committed). An early dip (\(t<1\) Myr, before any SN) in 5/6 configs: cooling \(L_{\text{loss}}\) rises 2–20× while \(L_{\text{gain}}\) stays flat (the bubble briefly becomes radiative), pulling the ratio down — the same first cooling episode the legacy solver transitioned on. Then a recovery: \(L_{\text{loss}}\) collapses (the bubble expands, \(n^2\Lambda\) falls) while \(L_{\text{gain}}\) is still flat — pure cooling, with supernovae absent (\(L_{\text{mech,SN}}\!\approx\!0\) until \(\sim\!3\) Myr). Only the later plateau is SN-sustained. So the early dip-then-surge is an \(L_{\text{loss}}\) story; the late surge is the feedback one.

Two views of one effect. (a) The metric is instantaneous: every time a feedback source switches on (Wolf–Rayet winds, then SNe) the \(L_{\text{gain}}\) denominator spikes, so the ratio jumps away from 0.05 exactly when cooling might catch up — it tests an instantaneous numerator, not an integrated budget. (b) hybr finds the true root: legacy clamped the cooling parameter \(\beta\!\in\![0,1]\) so \(P_b\) could only decline and the ratio drifted to balance; hybr is unbounded with a physical \(dM/dt>0\) gate and in places returns negative \(\beta\) (\(P_b\) rising — the bubble re-pressurising under a feedback surge), keeping the interior net-heating.

β alone vs β+δ — the right compression variable
A correction worth stating plainly (per the archived β–δ study): the structural quantity that governs the interior velocity is not β alone, and not β+δ=0. The bubble velocity ODE source term is \((\beta+\delta)/t = -t\,d\ln n/dt\), so β+δ is the compression term and its interior-inflow trigger sits at β+δ ≲ −0.4 (−0.5 for the steep archive config). β<0 by itself is only re-pressurisation (\(P_b\) rising); δ>0 (T rising) partly cancels it. In our six, β dives to −1.6 (re-pressurisation in 5/6), but β+δ crosses −0.4 in only one (large_diffuse, 10 rows) — everywhere else δ offsets β and the net compression stays above the trigger. The archive's resulting inflow is in any case “real but cosmetic” (subsonic, ~10⁻⁶ of thermal), not an energy-budget term — so this corrects the diagnostic framing, not the trigger verdict.

So what actually triggers a surge — feedback, or the structure (\(\beta,\delta\))? We measured both, per config, making no assumptions (step-to-step correlation of the ratio change \(\Delta r\) with \(\Delta L_{\text{mech}}\), \(\Delta\beta\), \(\Delta\delta\), over the implicit rows of each data/c0_*_h0.csv). The answer is the same in all six: the surge co-moves with a feedback power increase (\(\mathrm{corr}(\Delta r,\Delta L_{\text{mech}})>0\), +0.29 to +0.81) and with \(\beta\) dropping toward re-pressurisation (\(\mathrm{corr}(\Delta r,\Delta\beta)<0\), down to \(-0.89\)) — the two are co-driven by the same WR/SN episode (early \(\sim\!0.1\!-\!0.6\) Myr for midrange / simple_cluster, the SN onset \(\sim\!3\) Myr for the other four). But it is not a fixed \(\beta\) or \(\delta\) threshold: at the largest jump in each config \(\beta\) spans \(-0.2\) to \(+2.3\) and \(\delta\) both signs — there is no “\(\beta=-0.05\)” trigger value. Negative-\(\beta\) episodes do carry an elevated ratio (higher in \(\beta<0\) rows than \(\beta\ge0\) in 5/6 configs), so re-pressurisation is a co-symptom of the surge, not its threshold.

the fork this sets up
Two honest readings, both kept open: either the criterion needs replacing for the hybr regime (a trigger problem), or the bubble retains too much energy to ever balance because the cooling physics is incomplete (a physics problem). The rest of the report decides which — against external data, not against TRINITY's own assumptions.

2 · Test the different ideas — the method matrix

Before touching the trigger we drew a trust boundary: do not assume the substrate (the hybr solver and the \(\beta,\delta,P_b,E_b\) machinery the ratio reads) is correct — certify it with a cheap, independent gate first, and anchor the verdict to external physics (Lancaster+2021, El-Badry+2019, Geen+2021, Pabst+2020), treating TRINITY / WARPFIELD / Weaver as hypotheses, not ground truth.

Five candidate trigger families were on the menu — which fires when was to be measured, not inherited (a prior pass had leaked verdicts, so all prior firing-epochs/rankings were quarantined and re-derived from scratch):

triggerfamilycriterionidea being tested
rate-ratio CURRENTinstantaneous rate-ratio\((L_{\text{gain}}-L_{\text{loss}})/L_{\text{gain}} < \varepsilon\)the production baseline
cumulative coolingcumulative energy\(\int\!L_{\text{loss}}\,dt / \int\!L_{\text{gain}}\,dt > 1-\eta\)remove the instantaneous-reset artifact
cooling timescaletimescale\(t_{\text{cool}}/t_{\text{dyn}} < k\)classic Mac Low–McCray criterion
force balanceforce / continuity\(4\pi R^2 P_b /\,(\text{surviving forces}) < O(1)\)thermal drive becomes subdominant
blowoutblowout (geometric)\(R_2 > r_{\text{cloud}}\)shell escapes the cloud
mixing-layermixing-flux balanceLancaster+2021 fractal-interface coolingthe root-physics reading (no sharp 1D switch)

Each family was harvested per implicit-phase segment across the full span and judged against an independent oracle that depends on no candidate and no threshold: the \(E_b\)-peak (PdV-inclusive net-energy zero crossing, \(L_{\text{gain}}-L_{\text{loss}}-4\pi R_2^2 v_2 P_b \le 0\)).

the path was not straight (kept in the story)
A mid-run read at \(t\!\lesssim\!3\) Myr reported “no negative \(\beta\) in any config” — retracted once the runs passed the SN epoch, where negative-\(\beta\) re-pressurisation appears in all six (textbook “don’t trust one time-slice”). And the appealing cumulative-cooling fix — “the problem is just the instantaneous numerator, integrate it away” — was falsified: the cumulative ratio never fires either.

3 · Work the chosen reading across the configs

The decisive measurement is the external-anchored one: the retained-energy fraction \(f_{\text{ret}}(t)=E_b/\!\int\!L_{\text{mech}}\,dt\), run across all six regimes — spanning 3 dex in cloud mass, every density profile (flat, steep \(r^{-2}\), Bonnor–Ebert), and the full sfe range. If \(f_{\text{ret}}\) curves into the observed 0.01–0.1 band, the trigger question is well-posed; if it plateaus far above, the stall is an under-cooling physics gap.

The verdict holds across every regime, not just the easy one — including the steep \(r^{-2}\) crux, where \(L_{\text{loss}}\!\propto\!n^2\) collapses as the bubble expands into thin gas, giving it the highest retention (least cooling) of the span. The under-cooling is structural, not a one-config fluke.

configmCloud / profile\(f_{\text{ret}}\) end \(f_{\text{ret}}\) minin 0.01–0.1 band?
large_diffuse_lowsfe1e7 · flat0.2480.248no
be_sphere1e6 · Bonnor–Ebert0.2830.165no
midrange_pl01e6 · flat0.3300.169no
pl2_steep crux1e6 · \(r^{-2}\)0.3390.197no
small_dense_highsfe1e4 · flat0.3830.150no
simple_cluster1e5 · flat0.3970.150no

Numbers read from the final / minimum f_ret rows of each data/c0_*_st6.csv. Observed band: Lancaster+2021 (I&II), Geen+2021, Pabst+2020 (refs in FINDINGS.md §5).

4 · The measurements — where each family would fire

One row per config, harvested from data/c0_*_h0.csv (the G0 deliverable). The cooling and force families are flatly never; the cooling-timescale trigger is an artifact at \(t\!\approx\!0\); the \(E_b\)-peak oracle exists in only one config; and the blowout epoch tracks cloud size, not any cooling event — spanning 0.01 Myr (a 0.33-pc cloud) to 3.66 Myr (an 88-pc cloud).

config\(r_{\text{cloud}}\) [pc] rate-ratio / cumulative / forcetimescale (artifact)\(E_b\)-peak blowout [Myr]
small_dense_highsfe0.33never0.00—0.01
simple_cluster1.69never0.00—0.09
midrange_pl08.53never0.01—0.39
be_sphere15.50never0.01—0.86
pl2_steep21.40never0.00—0.84
large_diffuse_lowsfe88.00never0.395.063.66

Firing epochs from harvest_h0.py over data/c0_*_h0.csv. A structural note from the same harvest: \(P_b \equiv P_{\text{HII}}\) to machine precision (bubble–shell pressure continuity by construction, with \(P_{\text{ram}}=0,\,F_{\text{ISM}}=0\)), so any pressure-balance force criterion is degenerate — another reason no force criterion bites.

measurement verdict (6/6, unanimous)
For these under-cooled bubbles the energy→momentum transition is not a cooling / energy / force event at all — no scalar threshold in the rate-ratio / cumulative / timescale family can express it. The only physical end-of-energy-phase is geometric blowout, which is profile/size-dependent. The rate-ratio is not mis-tuned; it tests for an event that does not occur in the hybr regime.

5 · Solution — the diagnosis, and what (didn’t) ship

The deliverable here is a diagnosis, and a deliberate non-change to production. Retuning the 0.05 threshold is futile — there is no cooling-balance event in the hybr regime to tune toward.

recommendation
Pragmatic interim (if completable runs are needed now): a profile-aware blowout transition (\(R_2>r_{\text{cloud}}\)) — the only candidate that fires physically (caveat: its epoch is geometric, near-instant for compact clouds, so consider \(R_2>k\,r_{\text{cloud}}\) or a sustained criterion). Root fix: integrate Lancaster/El-Badry mixing-layer cooling so a cooling transition exists at all.

We prototyped the root fix far enough to size it. Offline (mixcool_whatif.py) a mixing-layer sink \(L_{\text{mix}}=\theta\,L_{\text{mech}}\) at the literature \(\theta\!\approx\!0.25\) brings \(f_{\text{ret}}\) into the observed band in all six configs — the direction is right.

retracted — the naive implementation does not work
Injecting that sink dynamically (subtracting \(\theta L_{\text{mech}}\) from \(dE_b/dt\) after the \((\beta,\delta)\) structure solve) is numerically non-viable: it drives the conductive \(dM/dt<0\) (no physical evaporation root), so hybr finds no root, the timestep-shrink guard spins, and the solver stalls. A proper mixing-layer cooling must be solved inside the structure solve (so \(\beta,\delta\) are found with it, keeping \(dM/dt>0\)) — a deeper change than a bulk energy sink. The prototype was reverted.
what shipped to production
Nothing. The bulk-sink injection and its mixL_theta param were reverted; trinity/ is byte-identical to its pre-prototype state and pytest is green (557 passed). The substrate stays frozen; the investigation closed the trigger question against external physics without a production change.

6 · The rest of the arc — the validation journey

The verdict only stands because the substrate was certified first, before any physics claim. The certification gate (C0) is independent of the contested trigger:

config\(\mathrm{res}_\beta\) median\(\mathrm{res}_{T_0}\) median neg-\(\beta\) rowssubstrate
large_diffuse_lowsfe4.66%0.13%9.3%sound
simple_cluster5.70%0.00%11.5%sound
small_dense_highsfe3.84%0.00%12.0%sound
midrange_pl06.08%0.00%4.3%sound
pl2_steep6.08%0.00%4.6%sound
be_sphere5.42%0.00%1.1%sound

The certification followed the project’s equivalence discipline: a per-segment residual is necessary but not sufficient, so res_beta was cleared with a 4× timestep-refinement check (median 6.65%→1.74%, scaling \(\propto\!\Delta t\) — the signature of truncation, not a bug), across the stiffest edge regimes (the steep \(r^{-2}\) crux included), in separate full runs at matched simulation time. The analytic adiabatic-Weaver null was infeasible (the solver cannot run with \(L_{\text{loss}}\!\equiv\!0\)), so substrate trust rests on the internal residuals plus the energy-fraction corroboration (the code reproduces Weaver 5/11, then stalls there — precisely the energy-conserving limit the literature says under-cools).

one-line takeaway
When a “stall” appears after a solver upgrade, certify the substrate before tuning the threshold: here the upgrade was correct and the stall was the model honestly reporting that its bubble never cools enough to transition — a physics gap, not a trigger bug. Don’t fix missing physics by tuning a scalar.

7 · Follow-up — legacy-vs-hybr, the dip mechanism, and the WARPFIELD / θ critique

7.1  Why the stall is new: BEFORE (legacy) vs AFTER (hybr)

The early dip is the first cooling episode. Under the legacy clamped-\(\beta\) solver the ratio is driven all the way down to 0.05 there and the run transitions to momentum; under hybr the same episode dips but recovers (\(L_{\text{loss}}\) collapses) and never crosses. That is exactly why the stall appeared only after the solver upgrade.

7.2  What the dip actually is: an emission-measure turnover, not a thermal trigger

We tested the obvious hypothesis — that \(L_{\text{loss}}\) rises because gas enters the efficient-cooling band (the \(\Lambda(T)\) peak, \(\sim\!10^5\!-\!10^6\) K) — and the data refuted it. \(T_0\) stays at 3–8\(\times10^6\) K through the whole dip and never enters that band, so \(\Lambda\) is effectively flat. The dip is set purely by the emission measure \(n^2 V \propto (P_b/T_0)^2 R_2^3\): it rises because volume growth outruns dilution, then collapses because \(R_2\) expansion dilutes \(n^2\) faster than \(V\) grows (the log-slopes cross at the \(L_{\text{loss}}\) peak in all six configs; the EM proxy peaks within 1.3× in time of the real \(L_{\text{loss}}\) peak).

the mechanistic root of the under-cooling
The bubble retains too much energy because its interior stays too hot (\(>\!10^6\) K, up in the weak-\(\Lambda\) bremsstrahlung tail) — it never makes the \(\sim\!10^5\!-\!10^6\) K gas that radiates efficiently. So the fix is not “add a loss term” but “create the cool, efficiently-radiating gas” — precisely what a turbulent mixing layer does. The dip diagnosis and the mixing-layer root-fix are the same story from two directions.

7.3  The WARPFIELD criterion is our current trigger in disguise

A WARPFIELD-style switch \(\log_{10}L_{\text{mech}}-\log_{10}L_{\text{cool}}<0.05\) (i.e. \(L_{\text{cool}}>0.89\,L_{\text{mech}}\), PdV excluded) is the same family as the current rate-ratio trigger. On our hybr data it does not fire: the gap bottoms at 0.145–0.292 dex (\(L_{\text{cool}}\) reaches only 51–72% of \(L_{\text{mech}}\), never 89%). The looser 0.89 threshold gets closer at the dip but the under-cooling wall still blocks it — the log-space reformulation doesn't escape the G0 verdict.

configmin WARPFIELD gap [dex]max \(L_{\text{cool}}/L_{\text{mech}}\)fires (<0.05 dex)?
small_dense_highsfe0.1450.717no
simple_cluster0.1700.676no
midrange_pl00.1970.636no
large_diffuse_lowsfe0.2720.535no
be_sphere0.2770.529no
pl2_steep0.2920.511no

The one real difference is WARPFIELD's leakage term \(L_{\text{cool}}=L_b+L_{\text{leak}}\); our default runs are sealed (\(\text{coverFraction}=1,\ L_{\text{leak}}=0\)). Leakage is an extra loss channel — whether a plausible coverFraction closes the 0.15–0.3 dex gap and stays solver-healthy is under test (Cf = 0.99 / 0.95 / 0.90). Note leakage vents hot gas; it does not create cool radiating gas, so it is a different lever than mixing-layer cooling.

7.4  Open problems with the \(\theta\,L_{\text{mech}}\) mixing-layer term

(1) double-counting against L_cool
TRINITY's \(L_{\text{cool}}=L_{\text{bubble}}+L_{\text{conduction}}+L_{\text{intermediate}}\) already contains a smooth 1-D interface model. Lancaster's \(L_{\text{mix}}\) is the same interface done turbulently, so adding \(\theta L_{\text{mech}}\) on top of the full \(L_{\text{cool}}\) double-counts the conductive/intermediate interface. The correct move is to replace \(L_{\text{conduction}}+L_{\text{intermediate}}\) with the turbulent term (or add only the excess) — not stack a flat fraction on the whole.
(2) θ=const carries no state dependence
\(L_{\text{cool}}=\int n^2 \Lambda(T)\,dV\) responds to the actual bubble structure; \(\theta L_{\text{mech}}\) knows only the feedback. \(\theta\!\approx\!\)const is an emergent 3-D-sim result in the efficient-cooling limit — it should depend on density, metallicity, turbulent velocity, density contrast. A constant \(\theta\) shifts every config equally and so cannot reproduce the measured config-to-config spread (\(f_{\text{ret}}\) 0.25–0.40). It is magnitude-right (it did bring the bubble into the band) but not predictive; a faithful term ties \(L_{\text{mix}}\) to interface area (\(\propto R_2^2\)), a mixing velocity, and the contact-discontinuity density, reducing to \(\theta L_{\text{mech}}\) only in the efficient limit.

7.5  What actually changed legacy→hybr: the β-clamp, not the temperature

Running the dip diagnostic on the legacy runs and overlaying them on hybr isolates the difference. At matched times the interior temperature \(T_0\) is ~identical in both (4–5\(\times10^6\) K) — the temperature is not what changed. The difference is entirely in β: legacy clamps \(\beta\!\in\![0,1]\), which forces a structure that keeps \(L_{\text{loss}}\) high so the ratio is driven monotonically down to its crossing; hybr is unbounded and β swings to +2…+4 right at the dip, the structure \(L_{\text{loss}}\) collapses, and the ratio recovers.

so the BEFORE/AFTER is a solver-root difference, not a cooling-physics one
The C0 certification established hybr finds the true root; legacy, when the true root leaves \([0,1]\), is pinned to the constraint edge. So legacy's “transition” was a consequence of the β-clamp (a constrained edge-root that keeps \(L_{\text{loss}}\) high), not genuine extra cooling — the interior is equally hot in both. This closes the loop with §7.1–7.2: the stall is what the correct root does, and the old trigger fired only because the caged solver couldn't reach it.

Artifacts & reproducibility

Everything is committed under docs/dev/transition/cleanroom/ — reproducible without re-running the (hours-long) hybr sims. Each figure is a pure read of a committed CSV.
